page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.g5ne.2xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine instance powered by the KVM hypervisor. It features 8 dedicated vCPUs on an x86_64 architecture, utilizing an Intel Xeon 8163 processor running at 2.5 GHz. The instance is configured with 32.0 GB of system memory, providing 4.0 GB of RAM per core. It does not include local storage or dedicated GPU hardware, and it offers a baseline network bandwidth of 4 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks indicate that the ecs.g5ne.2xlarge sits primarily in the lower-to-average performance tiers. Its single-core and multi-core CPU performance, as measured by stress-ng and Geekbench, falls into the bottom 25%. However, it achieves average performance in large-block memory bandwidth reads and writes, as well as in Passmark CPU and extended instruction tests. Cryptographic and compression tasks show weaker results, with single-threaded gzip compression scoring in the bottom 10%. For lightweight workloads, it handles Redis SET operations and small-model LLM inference at average speeds, though performance drops significantly for larger models.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this instance offers a dedicated resource allocation without the premium of high-performance compute or memory-optimized classes. The lack of bundled local storage means storage costs are decoupled, allowing users to attach network storage as needed. Given its balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio and moderate processing capabilities, the ecs.g5ne.2xlarge is best suited for general-purpose workloads such as moderate web serving, basic database operations, and lightweight application hosting that do not require intensive single-core performance or local SSD speeds.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.g5ne.2xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring 8 dedicated vCPUs powered by an Intel Xeon 8163 processor running at 2.5 GHz on an x86_64 architecture. It is equipped with 32.0 GB of RAM, providing a balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio, but includes no local storage or GPU accelerators. Operating on the KVM hypervisor, it delivers a baseline network bandwidth of 4 Gbps. Benchmark data shows weak single-core and multi-core CPU performance, placing it in the bottom 25% for stress-ng and Geekbench tests, though it maintains average performance for large-block memory bandwidth and Redis SET operations. Given its dedicated CPU allocation and moderate performance profile, this instance is qualitatively cost-efficient for steady-state, non-burstable workloads. It is best suited for general-purpose applications, moderate web serving, and lightweight database operations that do not demand high-speed local storage or cutting-edge processing power.
